questions.question: What is the 'Markov property' of a stochastic process?
options: The next state depends only on the current state, not on the history
questions.explanation: Markov property: `P(X_{t+1} | X_t, X_{t-1}, ..., X_0) = P(X_{t+1} | X_t)`. Past is irrelevant once you know the present. This 'memorylessness' is what makes the transition matrix capture everything you need to analyze the chain.

questions.question: How do you compute the n-step transition probabilities of a Markov chain with matrix P?
options: P^n (the matrix raised to the nth power)
questions.explanation: (P^n)_{ij} is the probability of being in state j after n steps given start at i. Use binary exponentiation (Phase 01 Lesson 14) to compute P^n in O(d³ log n) for a d-state chain.

questions.question: A stationary distribution π satisfies which equation?
options: π · P = π (π is a left eigenvector of P with eigenvalue 1)
questions.explanation: π · P = π — a row vector π satisfies the equation as a left-multiplied eigenvector. For ergodic (irreducible + aperiodic) finite chains, π is unique and any initial distribution converges to it geometrically fast.

questions.question: What's the stationary distribution of a simple random walk on an undirected graph G = (V, E)?
questions.explanation: π_v = deg(v) / (2|E|). High-degree vertices are visited more often. For regular graphs (all degrees equal), this reduces to uniform. This is the foundation of PageRank: high in-degree pages get higher stationary mass.

questions.question: Why does PageRank add a damping factor (1 - α) / n?
options: It ensures the chain is irreducible and aperiodic (the 'teleport' guarantees every page can reach every other) — needed for the chain to have a unique stationary distribution.
questions.explanation: Without damping, dangling pages (no outlinks) or disconnected webs would break ergodicity — power iteration wouldn't converge to a unique limit. Damping = 'teleport to a random page with probability 1 - α' makes the matrix strictly positive, guaranteeing ergodicity. α = 0.85 is empirical.

questions.question: Mixing time of a random walk is controlled by:
options: The spectral gap (1 - λ₂) of P — larger gap means faster mixing; expander graphs have constant gap → O(log n) mixing
questions.explanation: Mixing time ~ (1 / spectral_gap) · log(1/ε). Expanders (random regular graphs, Ramanujan graphs) have spectral gap bounded below by a constant → O(log n) mixing. Cycles have gap O(1/n²) → slow O(n² log) mixing. This drives the entire theory of MCMC sampling.
